content.text: A cost centre is an organisational unit that is accountable for specific Azure spending. Finance teams use cost centres to allocate cloud bills to business units, products, or projects — the same way they allocate office rent or payroll. In Azure, you implement cost centre organisation through a combination of subscription structure, resource group layout, resource tags, and management group hierarchy.

The goal is traceability: every dollar spent in Azure should be attributable to a known cost centre, enabling accurate chargebacks, showbacks, and budget conversations with the teams that own the spending.

attrs.alt: Three patterns for cost centre organisation in Azure: per-subscription, tags in shared subscription, and hybrid

content.text: Pattern A: Subscription per Cost Centre

The cleanest boundary is a dedicated subscription per cost centre. Azure's billing boundary is the subscription, so a cost centre subscription receives its own invoice, its own budget scope, and its own cost view. There is no ambiguity about which team owns which costs.
Pros: billing is naturally separated; budgets and alerts are subscription-scoped; RBAC isolation is free.
Cons: subscription sprawl — an organisation with 50 cost centres needs 50 subscriptions minimum, which multiplies management overhead (policy, RBAC audits, security baselines).
Best for: large departments or products that each have substantial spend (>$2,000/month) justifying the overhead of a dedicated subscription.
Pattern B: Resource Tags in a Shared Subscription

Tags on resources and resource groups let Cost Management group and filter costs by any dimension, including cost centre. A single subscription can host resources belonging to many cost centres if every resource carries a CostCentre tag.
Pros: fewer subscriptions to manage; works well for small organisations or projects with modest spend.

Cons: shared resources (VNet, DNS, Log Analytics workspace) are hard to tag to a single cost centre; untagged resources create attribution gaps; policy enforcement is needed to prevent untagged deployments.
Best for: small-to-medium organisations or individual product teams that share infrastructure.

The most common enterprise approach is one subscription per department or major product line, with tags used inside each subscription to attribute costs to sub-teams or projects. This limits subscription count while still providing sub-department visibility.
Pros: department-level billing boundary (subscription) plus fine-grained attribution (tags); manageable number of subscriptions.
Cons: shared platform subscription costs (networking, monitoring, security) must be apportioned across cost centres manually.
Best for: mid-to-large organisations with clearly defined departments, each with several teams.
A consistent tag taxonomy applied to all resources is the foundation of cost centre organisation. Define the taxonomy before resources are deployed and enforce it with Azure Policy.

# Apply standard cost attribution tags to a resource group
az group update \
  --name "rg-payments-prod" \
  --tags \
    CostCentre="CC-1234" \
    Department="Engineering" \
    Team="Backend" \
    Application="payments-api" \
    Environment="prod" \
    Owner="backend-team@contoso.com"

# Apply tags to an individual resource (inherit from RG or override)
az vm update \
  --name "payments-vm-01" \
  --resource-group "rg-payments-prod" \
  --set tags.Application="payments-api" tags.Owner="alice@contoso.com"

# Bulk-tag all untagged resources in a resource group
az resource list --resource-group "rg-payments-prod" \
  --query "[?tags.CostCentre==null].id" -o tsv | \
  xargs -I{} az resource tag --tags CostCentre="CC-1234" --ids {}

Tags are only useful for cost attribution if they are consistently applied. Azure Policy deny effects block deployments that are missing required tags; modify effects automatically append tags to compliant resources.
Require CostCentre Tag on Resource Groups

# Assign the built-in policy: Require a tag on resource groups
az policy assignment create \
  --name "require-costcentre-tag" \
  --display-name "Require CostCentre tag on resource groups" \
  --policy /providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/96670d01-0a4d-4649-9c89-2d3abc0a5025 \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "CostCentre"}}'

# Assign the policy to deny any resource group created without the tag
az policy assignment create \
  --name "deny-rg-without-tags" \
  --display-name "Deny resource groups missing required tags" \
  --policy /providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/96670d01-0a4d-4649-9c89-2d3abc0a5025 \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --enforcement-mode Default \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "Department"}}'

# Check current compliance
az policy state summarize \
  --policy-assignment "require-costcentre-tag" \
  --query "{Total:results.resourceDetails[0].count,Compliant:results.resourceDetails[1].count,NonCompliant:results.resourceDetails[2].count}" \
  -o table

# Find non-compliant resource groups (missing CostCentre tag)
az policy state list \
  --policy-assignment "require-costcentre-tag" \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query "[].{Resource:resourceId}" -o tsv

# Deploy the inherit-tag policy
az policy definition create \
  --name "inherit-costcentre-from-rg" \
  --display-name "Inherit CostCentre tag from resource group" \
  --rules @inherit-costcentre.json \
  --mode Indexed

az policy assignment create \
  --name "inherit-costcentre" \
  --policy "inherit-costcentre-from-rg" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus \
  --role "Contributor" \
  --identity-scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>"
Once tags are applied consistently, Cost Management's grouping and filtering capabilities let you slice costs by any tag dimension.

# Current month cost grouped by CostCentre tag
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity None \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"TagKey","name":"CostCentre"}]' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o table

# Daily cost trend for a specific cost centre (last 30 days)
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --timeframe Custom \
  --time-period '{"from":"2026-06-01","to":"2026-06-30"}' \
  --dataset-granularity Daily \
  --dataset-filter '{
    "tags": [{"name":"CostCentre","operator":"In","values":["CC-1234"]}]
  }' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o table

# Multi-subscription cost by CostCentre (using management group scope)
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<mg-id>" \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity None \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"TagKey","name":"CostCentre"},{"type":"Dimension","name":"SubscriptionName"}]' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o table

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# cost-centre-report.sh — email a cost-by-cost-centre report at month end

SUBSCRIPTION_ID="<subscription-id>"
REPORT_DATE=$(date +%Y-%m)
OUTPUT_FILE="/tmp/cost-report-${REPORT_DATE}.json"

# Query cost by CostCentre for the previous month
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION_ID" \
  --timeframe BillingMonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity None \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"TagKey","name":"CostCentre"}]' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o json > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

# Parse and format the report
python3 << 'EOF'
import json
with open("/tmp/cost-report-$(date +%Y-%m).json") as f:
    data = json.load(f)

rows = data.get("properties", {}).get("rows", [])
cols = [c["name"] for c in data.get("properties", {}).get("columns", [])]

print(f"{'Cost Centre':<20} {'Cost (USD)':>12}")
print("-" * 34)
total = 0
for row in sorted(rows, key=lambda r: r[0], reverse=True):
    cc = row[cols.index("CostCentre")] if "CostCentre" in cols else "Untagged"
    cost = row[cols.index("Cost")]
    print(f"{str(cc):<20} {cost:>12.2f}")
    total += cost
print("-" * 34)
print(f"{'TOTAL':<20} {total:>12.2f}")
EOF

Shared platform resources — hub virtual networks, Log Analytics workspaces, Azure Firewall, DNS Private Zones — serve multiple cost centres but cannot be tagged to any single one. Two approaches handle shared costs.

Finance and the platform team agree on a fixed percentage split for shared costs. The platform subscription's total monthly cost is divided by those percentages and charged to each cost centre in the financial system (not in Azure itself).
Proportional Allocation: Based on Actual Usage

# Proportional allocation: split shared costs based on each cost centre's
# share of total billable resource cost

# Get total cost and per-cost-centre cost for the subscription
TOTAL=$(az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>" \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  --query "properties.rows[0][0]" -o tsv)

SHARED_PLATFORM_COST=850  # e.g., from the shared platform subscription

# For each cost centre, calculate their share of total spend
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>" \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity None \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"TagKey","name":"CostCentre"}]' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o json | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
rows = data['properties']['rows']
total = sum(r[0] for r in rows)
shared = $SHARED_PLATFORM_COST
print(f'Shared cost to allocate: \${shared:.2f}')
print(f'{"Cost Centre":<20} {"Direct":>10} {"Shared Alloc":>14} {"Total":>10}')
print('-' * 58)
for r in sorted(rows, key=lambda x: x[0], reverse=True):
    cc = r[1] or 'Untagged'
    direct = r[0]
    alloc = shared * (direct / total) if total else 0
    print(f'{cc:<20} {direct:>10.2f} {alloc:>14.2f} {direct+alloc:>10.2f}')
"
Cross-Subscription Cost Reporting with Management Groups

Management Groups let you apply cost policies and query costs across dozens of subscriptions as a single unit. A Cost Centre hierarchy maps naturally onto a management group tree: the top-level management group holds the EA enrollment, child groups correspond to business units, and leaf groups hold the subscriptions that belong to each cost centre.
Management Group Hierarchy for Cost Reporting
Create the hierarchy once and all new subscriptions are automatically included in the right scope's cost roll-up.

# Create management group hierarchy
az account management-group create \
  --name mg-corp-root \
  --display-name "Corp Root"

az account management-group create \
  --name mg-engineering \
  --display-name "Engineering" \
  --parent mg-corp-root

az account management-group create \
  --name mg-finance \
  --display-name "Finance" \
  --parent mg-corp-root

# Move subscription into the Engineering management group
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name mg-engineering \
  --subscription "sub-eng-prod"

# Query costs at the management-group scope (Engineering BU)
az costmanagement query \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-engineering" \
  --type Usage \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity Daily \
  --dataset-aggregation '[{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}]' \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"TagKey","name":"CostCentre"}]' \
  -o table
Budgets can be set at the management group level to enforce a ceiling for an entire business unit.

# Set a budget at the engineering management group scope
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name budget-eng-2024 \
  --amount 500000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2024-01-01 \
  --end-date 2025-01-01 \
  --category Cost \
  --resource-group-filter "" \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-engineering"

# List all budgets at that scope
az consumption budget list \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-engineering" \
  -o table
Chargeback and showback are the two dominant cost accountability models in enterprise Azure environments. They differ in whether the cost centre actually pays for its Azure consumption.
Teams see their Azure costs but the central IT budget pays
Teams are billed (internally) for their Azure costs
Low — read-only cost views per team
High — internal billing, GL entries, finance approval
Mature FinOps; product-led or profit-center teams
Implementing Showback with Cost Reader Role
Assign the Cost Management Reader built-in role at the subscription or resource-group scope for each cost centre owner so they see only their portion of the bill.

# Grant a cost-centre team lead read-only access to their subscription costs
PRINCIPAL="jane.smith@contoso.com"
SUB_ID="sub-cc-marketing"

az role assignment create \
  --role "Cost Management Reader" \
  --assignee "$PRINCIPAL" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUB_ID"

# Optionally at resource-group scope for shared subscriptions
RG="rg-marketing-prod"
az role assignment create \
  --role "Cost Management Reader" \
  --assignee "$PRINCIPAL" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/$RG"
Chargeback with Cost Exports to Storage
For true chargeback you export daily cost data to a storage account, load it into your finance ERP (SAP, Dynamics, Oracle), and raise internal invoices per cost centre each month.

# Create a cost export to a storage account (run once per subscription)
SCOPE="/subscriptions/sub-cc-marketing"
STORAGE_ID=$(az storage account show \
  --name stcostexports \
  --resource-group rg-billing \
  --query id -o tsv)

az costmanagement export create \
  --name export-marketing-daily \
  --scope "$SCOPE" \
  --type ActualCost \
  --storage-account-id "$STORAGE_ID" \
  --storage-container cost-exports \
  --storage-directory marketing \
  --recurrence Daily \
  --recurrence-period from="2024-01-01" to="2025-01-01" \
  --schedule-status Active \
  --dataset-granularity Daily

# Each day, marketing's CSVs land in:
#   stcostexports/cost-exports/marketing/<date>/
# ERP integration picks these up via Logic App or Azure Data Factory
Azure-native cost tools cover the basics but most enterprises connect Azure cost data to visualisation and BI platforms for richer cost centre dashboards.
Azure Monitor Workbooks for Cost Centre Dashboards
Azure Monitor Workbooks can query Cost Management and display parameterised charts that let users filter by CostCentre tag without needing Power BI licences.
Use the Azure Cost Management connector in Power BI Desktop to pull actual and budgeted spend. Slice by CostCentre tag to produce per-team P&L views.

# Export cost data for Power BI consumption
# 1. In Power BI Desktop: Get Data > Azure Cost Management
# 2. Scope: Management Group or Subscription
# 3. Granularity: Monthly
# 4. Fields to include: CostCentre (tag), ResourceGroup, ServiceName, Cost

# Alternatively, export to ADLS Gen2 and connect Power BI via Dataflow
az costmanagement export create \
  --name export-powerbi-monthly \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp-root" \
  --type ActualCost \
  --storage-account-id "$STORAGE_ID" \
  --storage-container powerbi-exports \
  --recurrence Monthly \
  --dataset-granularity Monthly \
  --schedule-status Active
Subscription-Level Budget Alerts per Cost Centre
Even in hybrid patterns (Pattern C) you can wire budget alerts per cost centre by combining a subscription budget with an action group that emails the cost centre owner.

# Create an action group that emails the CC owner
az monitor action-group create \
  --name ag-cc-marketing-owner \
  --resource-group rg-billing \
  --short-name ccmktg \
  --email-receiver name=owner \
    email-address=marketing-lead@contoso.com

# Create a budget with threshold alerts at 80%, 100%, 110%
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name budget-cc-marketing-2024 \
  --amount 120000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2024-01-01 \
  --end-date 2025-01-01 \
  --category Cost \
  --scope "/subscriptions/sub-cc-marketing" \
  --notification threshold=80 operator=GreaterThan \
    contact-emails='["marketing-lead@contoso.com"]' \
    contact-roles='["Owner"]' \
  --notification threshold=100 operator=GreaterThan \
    contact-emails='["cfo@contoso.com","marketing-lead@contoso.com"]' \
    contact-roles='["Owner"]' \
  --notification threshold=110 operator=GreaterThan \
    contact-emails='["cfo@contoso.com"]' \
    contact-roles='["Owner"]'

echo "Budget and alerts configured for Marketing cost centre"

Cost centre organisation in Azure maps business financial accountability to Azure's billing and access control constructs. The three main patterns are: a dedicated subscription per cost centre (clean boundary, higher subscription count), tags in a shared subscription (fewer subscriptions, requires strict tag governance), and a hybrid approach (one subscription per department, tags for sub-team attribution — most common in enterprises). A well-defined tag taxonomy with Azure Policy enforcement ensures every resource is attributable. Cost Management's grouping and filtering by tag let finance teams query actual spend per cost centre programmatically. Shared platform costs require a separate allocation strategy — either a fixed percentage split or a proportional split based on each cost centre's relative direct spend.
